question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer:
When the fork() system call is called, it creates a child new process which is exactly identical to the parent process. It copies all the values from the PCB of the parent to the B child except the pid which is different & the eax register which is the return value of the fork. In this copying, It also copies the trapframe of the parent process to the child process. So whenever, the child gets scheduled on the CPU, it reloads the trapframe of the parent and returns from kernel mode to the user mode via this registers.

Thus, although there was no trap in the child process, it return from kernel mode to user mode via the trapframe copy of the parent process stored in its PCB via initializing the child in the fork.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: The response touches upon the transfer of the trapframe from the parent to the child process. However, it does not provide the specific details of how the registers in the trapframe allow the child process to return from the kernel to the user mode. Try to delve deeper into the mechanics of the trapframe and the associated registers that facilitate this return.

reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.

question:

Note that the user process has to know specifically how to make such an attempt.
(e.g., the user process does not know that a PCB exists and hence may not know how to
update its contents).
student_answer:
A user process may attempt to modify variables outside its address space, which is avoided by using virtual memory and not allowing the process to modify or protect the rest of the memory being exposed.
A user process may attempt to read from a file which is either taking too long or doesn't have read permissions. question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer:
The child process on creation and after copying the content of parent, (also the eax register is changed for return value of fork) has it's status as "ready" and the scheduler can just schedule this process and it can run.
Note that the kernel stack of child process will also be copied.
Note that we can restore the trap frame and context from the PCB, as copied from parent and hence the return from trap works because of context trapframe & context
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: Your answer acknowledges some crucial points, such as the child process being ready to execute and the copying of content from the parent. However, to enhance clarity, consider mentioning how the child process context is set up specifically for execution in kernel mode. Additionally, elaborate on the process of the child process returning from the trap, explaining how it emulates the call and return from the trap handler.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer: When a fork() call is made the os first allocates a process using allocproc() and then copies various attributes like, open-files, context, etc. from the parent to the child. It also allocates space for the user-stack of the new process. However since there was no trap for the child process, it copies the parents trap-frame to the child and sets the eax in the trapframe to 0. Now since the new process has almost everything set up as the parent process and the os has also set up the trapframe, the child process can return from the trap using trapget and since eax was set to 0 we also get the value returned by fork to be 0 in the child process.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: The description of how the attributes are copied is good. You also correctly described the setting of 'eax' to 0 to set the return value of fork. However, you did not mention how the context for the forkret function is set for the child process. Elaborate on that aspect.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer:
Child process
If there is no specific task in child process, it gets executed completely and then the context switch happens to another process which is ready to get scheduled. So, it returns to user mode from kernel mode.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: Your answer lacks the essential details about the child process's setup and context switch. The reference answer elaborates on how the child process's context is set to initiate execution in kernel mode and return to user mode. For a more accurate response, consider addressing these key aspects.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer: In child process, there was no trap.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: A trap is not explicitly set for the child process. However, the context for the child process' execution is set up in a way that it appears as if a trap has occurred. This allows the child process to return to user mode.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer: When the fork() syscall is made, the parent creates a new process in the PCB by assigning memory, context, pid, etc in the kernel mode and once the child is created, the trap handler returns. Now a timer interrupt may occur. This interrupt makes the OS take control. Now the OS goes to the Ptable and decides on which process to run next. Now it may choose the child process and the user code in the child process starts running from the point after the fork() system thus timer interrupts that goes to the scheduler logic makes the child process run the user code.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: Your answer describes the scheduling mechanism of processes instead of the return path from the kernel mode. Review the concept of "return path from a trap" and how it relates to the creation of a child process.

question:

Allocating data directly by using sbrk/brk syscall instead of malloc() is a privileged action, hence prevented by the OS.

- Access to VA that lies in the kernel virtual address range

question:
a. List and explain two privileged actions that an user process may attempt to perform and which
are prevented by the OS via the limited-directed execution setup.
Note that the user process has to know specifically how to make such an attempt.
(e.g., the user process does not know that a PCB exists and hence may not know how to
update its contents).
student_answer:
1. A user process can try and read from a read-only file. This action will be prevented by the OS since in the LDE setup, it will ensure that the privilege level on the file matches the action.

- Access to VA that lies in the kernel virtual address range

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer: The issue described in the problem is identical to the issue faced in process creation. The fork syscall initializes the PCB entry for the child process and copies the necessary
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: Your answer is on the right track, but it doesn't directly address the question. The question asks about the specific mechanisms in the child process that allow it to return from the kernel to user mode without a trap, while your answer talks about process creation and copying PCB entries. Focus on explaining how the child process returns from the kernel to user mode without a trap.

When fork call is made by parent, a new pcb and memory are created by the os for the child identical to the parent process.
But, in the child process trapframe is designed so that forkret executes when the child process is scheduled and after fork return & child process is scheduled and after fork return the next addr. in the trapframe is the trapret function addr.
So, after child process is created with the above trapframe, the scheduler on the child will execute when the scheduler context switch to child process.
After switching to child, it executes forkret(), and then the trapret(), which will switch back from the kernel to the user mode.

Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function

set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer: The return path from kernel mode to the user mode for the child process returns -1 despite not having a trap returns because of scheduling. A new process is created and it is scheduled by the scheduler. There is no syscall like fork inside it though. The child also contains the pid of its parent process from which it was forked.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: Good thinking on the role of scheduling. However, the return from kernel mode to user mode is not a result of scheduling. It is a carefully crafted mechanism involving setup of the child process's kernel stack and a function (like forkret) to emulate the return from the trap handler.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer:
When fork() is called, all contents of the parent is copied to that of child. There is only one thing that is different between them and that is their k-stack. The task receives fd of child as return value while child receives 0.
For the child process, after the content and t are copied from its parent, the eip register value is set to 0.
The then pops through all register values and executes.
After that the child process returns from kernel mode to user mode with a return value of 0.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: Your answer covers some steps of the return process, but it's missing important details. The context for the child's execution is set up to initiate execution at the start of a function. The kernel stack is modified with the return address set as the return from the trap handler. This enables the child process to resume execution in the user mode.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer:
Ans: for exec() system call is made the child process
we are return the path handle mode. So we can
call for exec() system and handles memory by kernel 
mode user mode Trap/Interrupt the call system

Ans: Kernel mode in child process, that the return path
called by user mode first of all system call
child process going to fill directory and add the fill
Trap/Interrupt mode.
So child process calling the trap mode.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: Your answer has some misunderstandings. You mentioned the 'exec()' system call instead of 'fork()'. The return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process is set up by the kernel. The child process starts executing in a function and returns to the return address (the trap handler return address), emulating a call and return from the trap handler.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer:
For the parent process, it is handled by the
interrupt handler do return back to user mode.
For the child process, its trapframe is also
copied from the forked trapframe, thus whenever
it encounters a trap, it can be handled by the
trap handler. If we are talking about returning
just after the fork() call is made; then the
parent process is handled by the trap handler.
For the child process, we didn't erase it
running in the first place, so its setdata process
is simply put in runnable state where it can be
scheduled by the OS later on.
yield() function can also be used in case of semaphores
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: The provided response demonstrates a partial understanding of the topic. However, there are some inaccuracies and missed details. The explanation regarding the child process's trapframe is correct. But the mention of the yield() function and semaphores is not relevant to the context of the question. Focus on providing a more precise and detailed explanation that addresses the return path from kernel mode to user mode for the child process.

question:

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer: When a fork() system call is made, it does two main things. First, it sets the return value to zero for the newly created child process. It also copies the trapframe of the process that created the child process to the newly created child's trapframe. Second, it makes the trapframe PC point to a special function called folk_det(). So, when a newly created child is scheduled for the first time, it first goes to folk_set() function. In this function, the child processes will return to the user mode from the kernel mode.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: The provided response correctly identifies some steps of the process but misses key details. A trap handler return is emulated to return to user space, and the child process starts executing from a specific function (e.g., forkret). The rest of the kernel stack is then unrolled to complete the return to user mode.

set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer:
During handling of fork system call, the new PCB etc is allocated for the child process:
1. Create a new PCB with new pid & fill in process data
2. PCB contains the context for parent. In this context, the appropriate eip is written for the child
3. The trapframe inside the kernel stack is also updated with correct eip, esp ebp values. (eip comes from parent, eax, ebp are memory pointers after memory has been copied)
4. The return value register is written as 0 for child & pid of child for parent. Now both PCB and trapframe have been updated correctly. At this point, sched() is called to run the scheduled process.
Note that either parent or child are both runnable now.
The context information for child is present in a trapframe newly created by OS & stored in k-stack
If scheduler chooses to run child, it issues return from trap & the child's context is loaded from it's trapframe
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: Your response addresses the steps involved in preparing the child process for execution after the fork system call. However, it does not directly address the question of how the child process returns from a trap without having executed one. Elaborate on the specific mechanism that allows the child process to resume execution in user mode.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer:
During fork call, a new entry in PCB will now have its own set of registers, user stack, PC, etc.
After trap, it moves to scheduler and parent when scheduled, value of 'o' returned by fork function was stored in parent's register. Now space, also called as user space is moved to user area and loaded into parent's hardware.
Later, when child gets scheduled, child's own set of registers, PC, stack are restored after user space and made child run at exact point where parent called fork function.
So, on a new entry for child process is created in scheduler and parent run when either one gets scheduled respectively. Fork returns from trap and child has an entry in PCB from which it can be scheduled and run.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback:
- The answer correctly explains the creation of a new PCB entry for the child process and how registers are restored for the child when it gets scheduled.
- However, it does not address the specific question of how the child process returns from a trap without a trap occurring.

set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer:
The child process returns from a special trap return function called
forkret. The parent who creates the child sets the return program counter (eip)
to this place which then returns the child process to forkret.
From here, the child process returns back to user mode as if
it we returning from a trap instruction
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: You have correctly identified the function forkret as the starting point for the child process's return to user mode. However, your explanation could benefit from further clarification. Try to elaborate on the role of the parent process in setting the return program counter (eip) and how this action initiates the child process's return to forkret. Additionally, explicitly state the final step of returning to user mode after leaving the kernel stack.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer: When a fork() system call is made, it copies all the contents of the PCB of the parent-process to the PCB of the child process. PCB stores the information about trapframe also (address of the trapframe contents in the stack). The system call not just copies the contents of trapframe of parent-process to that in the child process, it also creates a new trapframe for the child process and copies the contents of trapframe of parent-process to that in the child process. This trapframe shows information about the trap including the return address. Thus the trap-handler utilizes the trapframe to make the child process return from a trap even though there was no trap in the child process.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: Your explanation acknowledges that a new trapframe is created for the child process and its contents are copied from the parent process. This is a key step in facilitating the return from the kernel mode to the user mode. However, there is no mention of the specific function (e.g., forkret) that the child process starts executing in the kernel mode, and how the return address is set to the trap handler return address. These details are important for a comprehensive understanding of the return path.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer: -> When the child process is in kernel mode, i.e. to return to the user mode, the parent makes a wait system call for the child. It waits for a particular child as there is no trap in the child process it cannot return back to the user mode, so when the parent calls wait system call for the particular children it waits in the kernel mode only, and the CPU schedule switches to schedule the different processes.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: The explanation incorrectly states that the parent process waits for the child process in kernel mode. The switch of CPU schedules to run a different process is not relevant to the return path of the child process.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer:
If there is no trap in the child process, then
it will return similar to the parent process.
when we fork() a process the child process
cannot copies identical data of the parent process
it also includes the parent trap frame.
and all those. So it will use same trap
frames to return from a trap.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: You are on the right track. The child process inherits the trap frame from the parent, but that's not the only reason it can return from a trap. Consider the setup of the child process's kernel stack and the role of specific functions like forkret and trapret in the return process.

question:
a. List and explain two privileged actions that an user process may attempt to perform and which
are prevented by the OS via the limited-directed execution setup.
Note that the user process has to know specifically how to make such an attempt.
(e.g., the user process does not know that a PCB exists and hence may not know how to
update its contents).
student_answer:
1) Creating a new process
Creating a new process using fork() is a privileged action. Creating a new process involves, among many other things, allocating space for a new PCB and populating it. The new process does not know about POS and thus cannot handle its creation on its own and has to request the OS to do so. Furthermore, the OS can prevent the process creation if, say, there is insufficient memory to create a new process.

2) Allocating memory on the heap
Allocating memory on heap (say, by malloc or calloc()) is also a privileged action. It requires finding free physical memory, mapping that memory to physical memory and updating the page table accordingly. The status of the physical memory and the directory of the process is not known by the user process and thus it cannot handle allocating a new page for heap from physical memory. It stays on missing and there needs to request the OS to do so. Furthermore, the OS can prevent the heap expansion if, say, there is insufficient physical memory for the same.
reference_answer:
- Write to CR3 register to modify page directory pointer
- Write to the EFLAGS registers to modify execution status (zero bit, interrupt enable/disable)
- Write to the IDTR register to change location of IDT
- Access to VA that lies in the kernel virtual address range
feedback: Your answer focuses on user processes' privileged actions. It does not discuss the OS-enforced prevention of these actions through limited-directed execution setup. You should consider adding information about how the OS stops these unauthorized attempts by restricting specific actions or memory areas.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer:
Child process returns from forkret function.
It returns from the same path as parent process, when stack is copied from parent to child content and trapframe are also copied hence child process also returns through trap as parent.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: Your answer captures the general idea of the return path for the child process. However, it would be more accurate to mention that the child process's execution starts at the forkret function and not directly from the trap handler like the parent process. Additionally, the transfer of control from the kernel mode back to the user mode is facilitated by the trap handler, which is not explicitly mentioned in your response. Try to be more comprehensive in your explanation next time.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: Consider the context setup for the child process in the kernel mode. There is a function (e.g., forkret) and a return address (e.g., trapret) setup in its kernel stack. This setup is what facilitates the emulation of a trap handler call and return.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer:
The fork system call copies the state of a process to a new process with different pid and parent pid (a diff return value of fork()).
There was no trap for child process, however all the state metadata about trap of parent process is copied onto the child process's state as well.
This is how the child process returns from trap (although with a different pid).
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: You identified that the child process inherits the trap state from the parent process. However, your answer lacks specific details about how the child process executes in the kernel mode and returns to user space. Try to focus on the return path mechanism as mentioned in the question.

question:

question:

Note that the user process has to know specifically how to make such an attempt.
(e.g., the user process does not know that a PCB exists and hence may not know how to

student_answer:
(i) Changing the interrupt-timer register value.
This is a register on CPU, but to update it requires privilege. It is important that no user process can modify it because if a user process modifies it then it can increase its value arbitrarily and gain full control of CPU (machine).
(ii) Changing the IDTR register value.
This is also a register on CPU and requires privilege to update. Suppose it was unprivileged then a process make it point to its own code somewhere, but on an interrupt, privilege level becomes 0 and the CPU will start executing the code from IDTR register.
reference_answer:

- Write to the EFLAGS registers to modify execution status (zero bit, interrupt enable/disable)
- Write to the IDTR register to change location of IDT
- Access to VA that lies in the kernel virtual address range
feedback: Excellent! You have correctly identified and explained two privileged actions that a user process may attempt to perform and which are prevented by the OS via the limited-directed execution setup. question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer: The context of the child process is saved in a register and the pointer which is initially pointed to this register of the child process is now shifted to the same register of another process in the kernel mode itself. (And assume that we created a new program in the user mode). Now this process in the kernel mode will return path to the user mode via trap/interrupt handler which now behaves exactly same as our previous child process.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: The provided explanation is incorrect. There is no direct register transfer from child to kernel process. The return path from kernel to user mode is emulated in the child process to behave like a return from a trap handler, enabling the execution of the child process in user mode.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer: The child process uses trap/interrupt handler of the parent process. As the parent and child use the same control block, so when child wants to make a system call or an interrupt, it goes via parent process -> trap handler, save registers into k-stack. Switch to privilege mode -> handle the system call -> return from trap -> restore registers from kernel stack. -> switch to user mode -> child process.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: You have the right idea about the child process using the parent's control block and trap/interrupt handler. However, there are a few key differences. Think about how the child process is set up to execute in kernel mode and how the return address is set in the child's kernel stack.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer:
On the kernel stack of child process the return address in set to "iret" so and the register of string the return value is also cleared to '0' so when the child process makes a "syscall" that it returns to given to the trapget and then execute the corresponding code + eventually return to usermode using "iret"

On the kernel stack of the child process we push the trapget. Their creates the illusion that the child process on completion of task goes to trapget. Them it executes the corresponding code + eventually return to usermode every time. Also the return value is set to '0'.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback:
- You have identified that the return address on the child process's kernel stack is set to "iret" and the return value register is cleared to '0'. This is correct.

- However, you have not explained how this allows the child process to return from a trap without having a trap in the first place. You need to provide more detail about how the child process is able to emulate a trap and return from it.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer:
The fork system call executes via an interrupt handler handling that system call in the kernel mode. While creating a new process, almost all the fields of the process are central block are the same except the pid and the virtual address space of both the parent and the child processes are initially the same. This means that the child is stored on the 8 trapframe. It also the same for the child process. The process state of the child process is marked as runnable and return value set to 0.

When the child process is scheduled, its kernel state is the same as it would be during a timer interrupt call (by setting kernel stack). It drops back to the trap handler section and then it drops back to the user state. While handling the times interrupt. Logic which is them responsible for switching the child process drops back to the user state. (Thus built setup creates an illusion of a process the child process returning from a trap).
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: You have understood the general concept of the return path from kernel mode to user mode for the child process. However, your answer could be improved by providing more specific details about the context setup for the child process, such as the function (e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack setup with the return address set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret). Including these details would enhance your explanation.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer: No picture
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: It seems like you didn't understand the question. The question is asking for the return path from kernel mode to user mode for the child process created by the fork() system call. It is not asking about depicting it using a picture. Try reading and understanding the question more carefully.

Then, when child gets scheduled, it starts running from task_entry using its(i.e, child) pointer) and goes zero initialization step of napping (limitation and then context switches back to user space using trap_return.

trap_frame has all the values like register values, eip, eax(to store return value), when child comes in user system, "looks" to it as if it has resumed its working post system call. whoever return value was 0.
we also change of trapframe and kernel ip and setting state IRUNNABLE results in proper forking.

- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.

feedback: The exec system call can be used in the parent process only after the child process terminates. This is because the child process inherits the file descriptors of the parent process, and the exec system call closes all open file descriptors. If the exec system call is used in the parent process before the child process terminates, the child process will inherit the closed file descriptors, which can lead to errors.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer:
- For the child process, the value of eax stored in the trap frame generated for it is explicitly set to 0, and otherwise the trap frame is identical to that of the parent.
- After fork finishes, the kernel calls the scheduler which may either call the parent or the child.
- Whatever is called, add the return from task_instruction to an executed offset on the processes trap frame, is then executed, then changing mode back to kernel mode
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: While your answer correctly mentions the value of eax being set to 0 for the child process, it does not explain the specific mechanism of how the child process returns to user mode. To improve your response, focus on describing the process of setting up the child's context and trap frame, and how that leads to the execution of the forkret function.

question: Differentiate between intermittent and ephemeral streams.
student_answer: Intermittent stream is a stream that has flowing water during certain times of the year, when ground water provides water for streamflow. During dry periods, these streams may not have flowing water. Run off from precipitation is supplemental source of water for stream flow. An Ephemeral stream has flowing water only during and for short duraton after precipitation events in a typical year. These are also referred as Streambed that are located above the water table year round and steams where ground water is not a source of water for the stream.
reference_answer: Intermittent stream is a stream that has flowing water during certain times of the year, when ground water provides water for streamflow. During dry periods, these streams may not have flowing water. Run off from precipitation is supplemental source of water for stream flow. An Ephemeral stream has flowing water only during and for short duraton after precipitation events in a typical year. question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer: on fork we create a PCA for child process with almost identical to parent process. In fork(), we point our induction pointers of childprocess to point to induction. we next initialize fdsr & and we return child process exactly like parent process i.e. by returning from trap handler of child process but because there is no pCA , trap-handling start all of that we have to create in folk and make sure our eip points to induction i.e next to folk, and copy stack content and all of that from parent process.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: Good! You mentioned the Parent Process Context Area (PCA) and induction pointer setup. However, you need to include more details about the specific steps taken to emulate the return from the trap handler, such as setting the return address in the child process kernel stack.

It is an trap occurred from the child process as well and the trap has has been completely successfully executed with return value of 0. The child then sets up all of its CPU registers while moving from kernel mode to user mode and "takes the value of function call" fork() to be 0. It also copied eip from parent, and hence continues it's execution from that point onwards.

- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.

set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.

for the child process
kernel mode to use mode
return path

process that reaps it
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.

question:

(e.g., the user process does not know that a PCB exists and hence may not know how to
update its contents).

1. The user process could attempt to modify the contents of a file which takes special privileges to access and edit. For instance, the OS would prevent arbitrary changes made to certain configuration files in /sys/ by some particular user via the limited direct execution setup.

2. The user process could attempt to access memory of another process, or worse, the kernel itself. The OS prevents this by performing checks and via the virtualization of memory.
reference_answer:
- Write to CR3 register to modify page directory pointer
- Write to the EFLAGS registers to modify execution status (zero bit, interrupt enable/disable)

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer: For the child process, during its creation of PCB, kernel stack there won't be any trap, so instead OS adds a return value 0 to the kernel stack of child process and changes it's state to runnable after adding it to the ptable. So now the child process don't need any trap handler to wake it. Scheduler normally runs this process.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: Your understanding of the child process's kernel stack setup is not precise. The purpose of adding a return value to the kernel stack is to return from the fork() system call, not to avoid the need for a trap handler. The OS does not change the child's state to runnable immediately after adding it to the process table; it waits for the child to be scheduled.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer:
fork() system call creates the child process by copying the whole virtual memory of the parent process onto another physical address and map these two. So basically the for child process uses the same trap table but different physical memory allocated to them. 

fork() call -> Allocates memory to the child process.
Copies the PC instruction pointer.
Copies the registers and also copies the trap state.
All the registers are stored on the kernel stack of child process.

When the child process is scheduled to run. Then the kernel transfers control from trap instruction and this wakes all the registers stored on the context of child to be given to the
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: You have correctly identified the key steps involved in the return path from kernel mode to user mode for the child process. However, your explanation could benefit from providing more specific details about how the context is set up for the child process to emulate a call and return from the trap handler. Try to elaborate on the role of the forkret function and the setup of the child process's kernel stack.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer:
when process calls fork() it uses the eax register to store the pid of child in parent & in child it is set to zero.
Every thing except this is copied or shared across both PCB's.
Now when this new child process is created it uses the trap frame of parent to create its new own trap frame & copies the return address from parent to itself.
when it returns from kernel mode of child process it uses this return address that it has loaded on stack it uses kernel stack to return to its user mode.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: Your understanding of the trap frame and the return address is good. However, you have not explained how the child process starts execution without being routed through a trap handler. Mention the function and the kernel stack setup provided within the reference answer.

question: Quote any one advantage of reservoir and channel flood routing each.
student_answer:
Reservoir Routing / Hydrologic Modelling- Uses continuity equation to find attenuated peak in case of flooding, so can be used in flood control.
Channel Routing / Hydraulic Modelling - Uses continuity as well as momentum(st venant equation) to find outflow at a channel c/s based on I1, I2, Q1 (c1,c2,c3) which is required very frequently (based on say Muskingum routing).

Reservoir Routing: Can be used to estimate peak reservoir levels during flood and hence helps in construction of overflow channels to avoid nearby areas to submerge during floods.

Channel Flood Routing: Helps in getting the flood-duration profile during a storm which helps in construction of bridges, transport control over channels, and considerations for bank-side development.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer:
A: * When fork() syscall is made, the child process is almost identical to the parent process.
* During fork(); allocproc is called where there are PCB is taken whose state is UNUSED then we update the PCB contents for this child process.
* For o updating, the state of the child process set to the RUNNABLE state and then we copy all the PCB contents of the parent process to the child process (except the tf -> eax is set to 0) Then the child process is returned.
* yield() is called where inside it the scheduler then there forkret is called and then the scheduler (i.e., scheduler) is called to run any other process to run.
* If any other process is set to run means the child process will be run after sometime by context switch between the running processes and the child process.
* During context switch the child process goes from kernel mode to the user mode and return-from-trap function is called where the child's kstack is restored and updated. The child is returned from forkret() so, the exact opposite to process of entering into the trap.

(users mode)
fork() (user mode)
forkret()
->
kernel mode?
(users mode)
child process
forkret()
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.

question: Differentiate between intermittent and ephemeral streams.

question:
c. When the fork() system call is made the child process is (almost) identical to the parent
process. In the parent process, the return path from kernel mode to user mode happens via
the trap/interrupt handler (system call is a trap/interrupt).
Explain the return path from the kernel mode to the user mode for the child process.
Specifically, there was no trap in the child process, so how does the child process return from
a trap?
student_answer:
The child process behaves as if it has been descheduled sometimes in past.
The fork system call sets the value of eax register (return value) in the stackframe of the child process to 0.
So, now when the child process is scheduled, it first sets the context in usual manner using the information stored in its stackframe. Chopping to CPU.
It stores the back the eip to point to the return address.
after the fork call, the eax register stores the return value and it first starts executing the instruction in usual sense. The eip of parent being stored in stackframe thus, does all the magic.
reference_answer:
- When a child process is created and is ready to execute, it is in the kernel mode.
Child process needs context to execute in the kernel mode.
- The context for execution of the child is setup to start execution at the start of a function
(e.g., forkret) and the kernel stack of the child process is setup such that the return address is
set as the return of the trap handler (e.g., trapret)
- As a result, when a child process in the kernel is scheduled, it starts executing in a function
and returns from it to the return address (the trap handler return address).
- This emulates a call to the trap handler and a return from the handler and then proceeds with
the rest of the kernel stack unrolling to return to user space.
feedback: Your answer is partially correct. You have identified the role of the eax register in storing the return value, but you did not fully explain the process of returning to the user mode. The reference answer provides a more detailed explanation of how the child process emulates a trap call and returns from the trap handler to complete its return to user space.
